fuzzy_prd: We need to complete the black-box command surface for the entity data-modeling toolkit, not only namespace signing. The package should preserve the behavior covered by the public cases: typed value normalization and validation for emails, phones, dates, names, identifiers, URLs, IPs, MIME types, JSON and countries; numeric/date casting and best-value selection; country inference; RDF term rendering; schema specificity and similarity; registry metadata; entity proxy add/set/remove/pop/first/merge/inverted behavior; deterministic entity and node IDs; graph adjacency; record mapping; JSON unpacking; provenance/name helper transformations; and namespace signing/verification. The adapter reads a command payload and emits deterministic key=value or line-based stdout. Namespace signing remains one feature, but the implementation must support the full command set.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with command/op and operation-specific fields) -> stdout for normalization, casting, entity, graph, RDF, helper, ID, mapping, and namespace-signing commands

trigger_keywords: what hashing algorithm is used for signing
which algorithm generates the stamp
how is the HMAC computed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The signing uses HMAC with SHA-1. The namespace string is the key and the value string is the message. The resulting hex digest is the signature. For example: namespace='banana', value='split' → signature='18375e11927c10b772d57d7535888545782bd7cf'.

trigger_keywords: what is the format of the signed output
how is the signed ID structured
how do you combine the value and the signature
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The signed form is the original value, a literal dot '.', then the hex signature: e.g., 'split.18375e11927c10b772d57d7535888545782bd7cf'. The output fields are 'signed' (the full dotted form) and 'signature' (the bare hex digest alone).

trigger_keywords: what happens when namespace is null or empty
behavior with no namespace secret
unsigned passthrough when namespace is missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the namespace is null (or not provided), the value is passed through unchanged as 'signed' and 'signature' is '<none>'. No HMAC is computed. Output: signed=<value>, signature=<none>.

trigger_keywords: what happens when value is null
signing a null value
behavior when the identifier to sign is missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the value to sign is null, both output fields are the null token. Output: signed=<none>, signature=<none>. This applies regardless of whether the namespace is set.

trigger_keywords: how does verification work
what does ns_verify check
when does verified return true
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: ns_verify checks whether the given value, when signed with the namespace, produces a signed form that matches itself. Since ns_verify receives an unsigned bare value (not the dotted signed form), it will always return verified=false for a plain identifier. Only a value that is already in the 'original.signature' dotted form AND whose signature matches would verify as true. Passing null also returns verified=false.

trigger_keywords: what are the output field names for signing
what keys does the sign command return
what is the stdout format for ns_sign
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The ns_sign command outputs exactly two lines: 'signed=<signed_value_or_none>' and 'signature=<hex_digest_or_none>'. The ns_verify command outputs one line: 'verified=true' or 'verified=false'. No other fields or error lines are emitted for these commands under normal operation.

trigger_keywords: how does the adapter normalize output
where is the output field naming convention defined
how do other commands format their results
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All command outputs follow the key=value line format used throughout the execution adapter. Null/absent results use the token '<none>'. Errors use 'error=<category>' lines. Look at how commands like 'normalize', 'entity_add', and 'make_id' are handled in the adapter layer for the exact pattern to follow.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

are there public test cases

can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'combine_names', 'filename', 'simplify_provenance', 'remove_prefix_dates', 'name_entity', 'person_cutoff', 'remove_checksums'. Each value maps to one feature.

trigger_keywords: followthemoney full command surface
not only namespace signing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The task includes the full public command surface: normalize, as_number, best_value, infer_country, rdf_term, entity_add, entity_set, entity_remove, entity_pop, entity_first, entity_merge, entity_inverted, make_id, node_id, graph_adjacent, map_record, json_unpack, helper, ns_sign, and ns_verify. Namespace signing is only one feature group.

trigger_keywords: followthemoney command dispatcher
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Route by top-level command for most cases. Some helper cases use command=helper with nested op values such as combine_names, simplify_provenance, filename, remove_checksums, remove_prefix_dates, name_entity, and person_cutoff. Preserve exact public-test stdout.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that token-signing approach we used in the old data-pipeline project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: HMAC-SHA1 signing as implemented in the namespace/signing module; see feature10_1_sign.json and feature10_2_verify.json test cases for the exact expected inputs and outputs

context_pointers.vague_reference: whatever normalization convention the rest of the adapter layer uses
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter's key=value line output format, with '<none>' for null/absent values and 'error=<category>' for errors, consistent with all other commands in the adapter (e.g., normalize, entity_add, make_id)

context_pointers.vague_reference: Check how similar commands are handled elsewhere in the codebase for the exact output field naming convention
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The output fields for ns_sign are specifically 'signed' and 'signature'; for ns_verify the field is 'verified'. These are defined by the test contracts in feature10_1_sign.json and feature10_2_verify.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more specific details on that aspect. Please refer to how the existing normalize and entity commands are structured in the codebase, and align with the same key=value output pattern used throughout the adapter layer. When in doubt, check the relevant test case files for the expected input/output contract.

feature10_1_sign.json.description: Signing tags an identifier with an HMAC computed from a namespace secret, producing a prefixed signed id and a bare signature; an empty namespace passes the identifier through unsigned.

feature10_2_verify.json.description: Verification accepts a correctly signed identifier and rejects an unsigned or null one.

feature11_adjacency.json.description: Loading entities into a property graph lets callers count the nodes adjacent to a given entity, where adjacency spans both relation edges and shared pivot values.

feature12_1_combine_names.json.description: Combining name parts produces alias values for every cross-product of first/patronymic/last name fragments.

cases.expected_output: alias=Vladimir Putin;Vladimir Vladimirovitch Putin;Wladimir Putin;Wladimir Vladimirovitch Putin

feature12_2_filename.json.description: A safe download filename is derived from an entity, deriving the extension from an explicit extension, MIME type or existing file name, with an optional override.

feature12_3_provenance.json.description: Provenance timestamps are simplified to a single earliest published date and a single modified date.

feature12_4_prefix_dates.json.description: Redundant low-precision dates are dropped when a more precise date sharing the same prefix is present.

feature12_5_name_entity.json.description: An entity is assigned a single canonical name chosen from its name candidates; a lone name is left unchanged.

feature12_6_cutoff.json.description: A person record is flagged when its birth date is so long ago that the person is assumed deceased; recent or already-dated records are not flagged.

feature12_7_checksums.json.description: Content-hash properties are stripped from an entity while other properties are retained.

feature1_10_topic.json.description: Topic tags are normalized by lowercasing and must belong to the controlled vocabulary; arbitrary words are rejected.

feature1_11_mimetype.json.description: MIME types are normalized by lowercasing and trimming; blank input is rejected.

feature1_12_ip.json.description: IP addresses are validated for both IPv4 and IPv6 structure; malformed octets or groups are rejected. Cleaning passes through valid addresses and rejects junk.

feature1_13_entity_ref.json.description: Entity reference identifiers accept digits, dashes and dots and may be extracted from an object carrying an id field, but reject values containing spaces or special characters.

feature1_14_identifier.json.description: Generic identifiers pass through largely unchanged but support fuzzy comparison that ignores a leading alphabetic prefix.

feature1_15_json.json.description: Arbitrary structured values are packed into a stable JSON wire string and can be unpacked back into the original structure.

feature1_1_email.json.description: Email addresses are normalized by lowercasing the domain while preserving the local part, stripping surrounding quotes, and rejecting malformed or over-long values; validation reports whether a value is a usable address.

feature1_2_phone.json.description: Phone numbers are normalized to E.164 form. A value without an international prefix can only be resolved when a region hint is supplied via an associated country; otherwise it is rejected.

feature1_3_url.json.description: URLs are normalized to a canonical absolute form: a path is added when missing and query parameters are reordered, while the host case is preserved. Validation accepts schemeless and protocol-relative hosts but rejects bare words.

feature1_4_iban.json.description: International bank account numbers are normalized by removing spaces and uppercasing. Validation enforces the checksum and per-country structure across many national formats.

feature1_5_name.json.description: Personal and organization names are normalized by collapsing internal whitespace and stripping surrounding quotes. Empty input is rejected by validation.

feature1_6_address.json.description: Postal addresses are normalized by flattening line breaks into comma-separated segments and collapsing whitespace, avoiding duplicated separators.

feature1_7_date.json.description: Dates are normalized to ISO-8601 partial form: numeric components are zero-padded, all-zero month/day segments are chopped back to the most precise meaningful prefix, and unparseable input is rejected. A custom input format can be supplied.

feature1_8_country.json.description: Country values are resolved to lowercase ISO codes. Known names (including historical states) are mapped to their codes, three-letter and unknown codes are rejected, and fuzzy name matching can be disabled.

feature1_9_language.json.description: Language values are resolved to three-letter codes; two-letter codes are expanded, and unknown codes are rejected.

feature2_1_number.json.description: Numeric strings are cast to floating-point values, tolerating grouping separators and sign spacing; non-numeric input yields no value.

feature2_2_date_number.json.description: Dates are cast to a numeric epoch-seconds value whose precision follows the precision of the input (year, month, day, hour, minute or second).

feature3_1_phone_country.json.description: A normalized international phone number yields a lowercase region code derived from its calling-code prefix.

feature3_2_iban_country.json.description: A bank account number yields a lowercase region code taken from its leading country segment.

feature3_3_country_hint.json.description: A country code is echoed as its own region hint.

feature4_1_rdf_term.json.description: Typed values render to stable, namespaced RDF term strings that encode the value's type as a prefix.

feature4_2_node_id.json.description: Pivotable typed values render to a graph node identifier string that is type-prefixed and case-normalized.

feature5_1_specificity.json.description: Specificity scores how informative a value is (0.0 to 1.0): a full date scores above a bare year, a longer name above a two-letter one, and unmatchable values score zero.

feature5_2_similarity.json.description: Similarity compares two values of the same type on a 0.0 to 1.0 scale: identical values score the maximum, a mismatch or a null operand scores zero, and identifiers match across a differing leading prefix.

feature5_3_best_value.json.description: Selecting the best value from a candidate list prefers the most informative form (e.g. the variant with the most capitalization signal), returning nothing for an empty list.

feature5_4_set_similarity.json.description: Set similarity compares two collections of values and returns a positive score when they share members, zero when one side is empty.

feature6_1_registry.json.description: The type registry resolves a type by name, reporting whether the name is known.

feature6_2_type_info.json.description: Each type exposes descriptive metadata: a stable machine name, a human label, and an optional grouping key.

feature7_1_add.json.description: Adding property values to an entity appends valid values, silently ignores null/blank input and duplicates, and reports a normalized error category when the property is not part of the schema.

feature7_2_set.json.description: Setting a property replaces all existing values with the given one, deduplicating repeated assignments.

feature7_3_remove.json.description: Removing a specific value drops only that value and leaves the rest of the property intact; removing an absent value is a no-op.

feature7_4_pop.json.description: Popping a property returns and clears all of its values at once; popping an empty property returns nothing.

feature7_5_inverted.json.description: An entity can be inverted into type-grouped value buckets and exposes a human-readable caption derived from its most salient name.

cases.expected_output:
caption=Ralph Tester
dates=1972-05-01
emails=info@ralphtester.me
identifiers=8e839023;9177171
names=Ralph Tester
phones=+12025557612
topics=role.spy
urls=https://ralphtester.me

feature7_6_merge.json.description: Merging one entity into another unions their property values when their schemas are compatible, and reports a normalized conflict category when they are not.

feature8_1_is_a.json.description: Schema descent can be queried: a schema reports whether it is the same as or derived from a named ancestor.

feature8_2_common.json.description: The closest common schema of two schemas is resolved where one exists; incompatible pairs yield a normalized error category.

feature8_3_descendants.json.description: A schema enumerates the set of schemas that derive from it (excluding itself).

feature8_4_matchable.json.description: Two schemas report whether instances of one may be compared against the other; document-like schemas are not matchable against legal entities.

feature8_5_reverse.json.description: A property exposes whether it is a stub (reverse-only navigation) relation and the name of its inverse property where one exists.

feature8_6_validate.json.description: Validating an entity payload against a schema succeeds for well-formed property maps and reports a normalized, per-property error category when a required value is malformed.

feature9_1_make_id.json.description: An entity derives a stable identifier by hashing its key fragments: the same input always yields the same id, a null fragment yields none, and an optional key prefix changes the result.

feature9_2_map_keys.json.description: A record-to-entity mapping derives each entity id by hashing the configured key columns (order-independent) together with an optional literal salt; records missing all key values produce no entity.

feature9_3_map_transform.json.description: Mapping property generators can join several columns into one value with a separator, or split a single column into multiple values.

feature9_4_map_links.json.description: A single record can yield multiple linked entities, where a relation entity references others by their mapping key.
